items.title: Severity criteria in asthmatic crisis
items.contentHtml:
Severity criteria in asthmatic crisis

  • Mild attack: normal or slightly increased respiratory rate, absent or minimal use of accessory muscles, oxygen saturation >95% and complete ability to speak.
  • Moderate attack: tachypnea, use of accessory muscles present, oxygen saturation 91-95%, short sentences and audible wheezing in expiration.
  • Severe crisis: respiratory rate >40-50 breaths per minute depending on age, intense suprasternal and intercostal indrawing, oxygen saturation <91%, inability to speak and audible wheezing on inspiration and expiration.
  • Imminent crisis (imminent arrest): bradypnea, auscultatory silence (absence of wheeze), cyanosis, bradycardia, hypotonia and altered sensorium; requires immediate intervention.

items.title: Modified Wood-Downes Scale
items.contentHtml:
Modified Wood-Downes Scale

  • Modified Wood-Downes Scale assesses the severity of the asthma attack in children from 1 to 5 years; total score from 0 to 10.
  • Rate 5 items: respiratory rate, wheezing, inspiration/expiration ratio, use of accessory muscles and heart rate.
  • Each item is scored 0, 1 or 2; score <5 indicates mild crisis, 5-7 moderate and >7 severe.
  • Key threshold: score >7 is associated with the need for admission to the pediatric intensive care unit and possible use of intravenous magnesium sulfate.

items.foco: Initial treatment with inhaled beta2 agonists
items.title: Initial treatment with inhaled beta2 agonists
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment with inhaled beta2 agonists

  • First line in asthmatic crisis: inhaled salbutamol using a metered dose inhaler with a spacer chamber or nebulization.
  • Dose in crisis: 2 to 4 puffs (100 mcg each puff) every 20 minutes for the first hour, or continuous nebulization of 0.15 mg/kg/dose (minimum 2.5 mg, maximum 5 mg) diluted in 3 mL of saline solution.
  • In severe crisis, continuous nebulized salbutamol can be administered at 0.5-1 mg/kg/hour for 1-2 hours.
  • Response evaluated after 3 doses: if there is no improvement, escalate to systemic corticosteroids and consider bromide ipratropium.

items.foco: Indications for systemic corticosteroids in crisis
items.title: Indications for systemic corticosteroids in crisis
items.contentHtml:
Indications for systemic corticosteroids in crisis

  • Indicated in moderate to severe crisis that does not respond to the first dose of inhaled salbutamol, or if the patient is already using maintenance inhaled corticosteroids.
  • Oral route is preferable over intravenous if the child can swallow; dose of oral prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day (maximum 60 mg/day) for 3-5 days, without the need for gradual reduction.
  • Intravenous route: hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg/dose every 6 hours or methylprednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/dose every 6 hours.
  • Onset of action: 4-6 hours; They should be administered as early as possible, ideally within the first hour of care.

items.foco: Use of ipratropium bromide in severe crisis
items.title: Use of ipratropium bromide in severe crisis
items.contentHtml:
Use of ipratropium bromide in severe crisis

  • IPratropium bromide is an anticholinergic that is added to salbutamol in severe or life-threatening crisis; should not be used as monotherapy.
  • Dose: 250 mcg (children <20 kg) or 500 mcg (children >20 kg) nebulized, mixed with salbutamol, every 20 minutes for the first 3 doses.
  • Reduces the risk of hospitalization and improves lung function in severe crises, especially in children 1-5 years.
  • Not indicated in mild or moderate crises; Its effect is maximum in the first hour and is not recommended beyond 3 consecutive doses.

items.title: Oxygen therapy and target saturation >94%
items.contentHtml:
Oxygen therapy and target saturation >94%

  • Oxygen therapy goal: maintain oxygen saturation >94% measured by pulse oximetry; in severe crisis or with hypoxemia, start with supplementary oxygen through nasal cannula or mask.
  • Initial flow: 1-2 L/min through nasal cannula, adjusting to reach saturation >94%; in young children, use a mask with a reservoir at 10-15 L/min if necessary.
  • Hypoxemia (saturation <90%) is a criterion of severity and is associated with a greater risk of respiratory arrest; If it persists despite oxygen, consider admission to the pediatric intensive care unit.
  • Do not use oxygen routinely if saturation >94% in room air; can delay detection of deterioration.

items.foco: Intravenous magnesium sulfate in severe crisis
items.title: Intravenous magnesium sulfate in severe crisis
items.contentHtml:
Intravenous magnesium sulfate in severe crisis

  • Intravenous magnesium sulfate is a second-line bronchodilator for severe or life-threatening crises that do not respond to beta2 agonists, corticosteroids and ipratropium bromide.
  • Dose: 40-50 mg/kg/dose (maximum 2 grams) diluted in 100 mL of 5% glucose solution, pass in 20-30 minutes.
  • Mechanism: calcium antagonist at the level of bronchial smooth muscle, reduces the release of acetylcholine and stabilizes mast cells.
  • Adverse effects: hypotension, facial flushing, drowsiness; monitor blood pressure and heart rate during infusion. Do not use if there is atrioventricular block or renal failure.

items.foco: Indications for intubation and mechanical ventilation
items.title: Indications for intubation and mechanical ventilation
items.contentHtml:
Indications for intubation and mechanical ventilation

  • Absolute indications: imminent respiratory arrest, auscultatory silence, bradypnea with severe hypoventilation, cyanosis despite 100% oxygen and altered sensorium (obtundation, coma).
  • Relative indications: progressive muscle fatigue, hypercapnia (partial pressure of carbon dioxide >55 mmHg) with respiratory acidosis (pH <7.25), and failure of maximum medical treatment.
  • Technique: orotracheal intubation with rapid intubation sequence; use ketamine as an inducer due to its bronchodilator effect.
  • Mechanical ventilation: use volume-controlled mode with low tidal volume (6-8 mL/kg), low frequency (10-15/min) and prolonged expiratory time to avoid air trapping.

items.foco: Classification of asthma in pediatrics by frequency
items.title: Classification of asthma in pediatrics by frequency
items.contentHtml:
Classification of asthma in pediatrics by frequency

  • Intermittent asthma: daytime symptoms <2 days/week, nocturnal awakenings <2 times/month, use of rescue salbutamol <2 days/week, without activity limitation.
  • Mild persistent asthma: daytime symptoms >2 days/week but not a daily, night awakenings 3-4 times/month, rescue use >2 days/week, mild limitation.
  • Moderate persistent asthma: daytime symptoms daily, night awakenings >1 time/week (not daily), rescue use daily, moderate limitation.
  • Severe persistent asthma: continuous daytime symptoms, frequent night awakenings (daily), rescue use several times a day day, severe limitation.

items.title: Maintenance treatment with inhaled corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
Maintenance treatment with inhaled corticosteroids

  • First line in persistent asthma of any severity: inhaled corticosteroids (e.g. budesonide, beclomethasone, fluticasone).
  • Low doses in children: budesonide 100-200 mcg/day or fluticasone 50-100 mcg/day; medium doses: budesonide 200-400 mcg/day; high doses: >400 mcg/day.
  • Administer with spacer chamber to improve pulmonary deposition and reduce oral effects; Rinse mouth after inhalation to prevent candidiasis.
  • Effect on growth: Prolonged high doses may cause mild growth retardation; use the lowest effective dose and reevaluate every 3-6 months.

items.foco: Therapeutic step according to pediatric GINA
items.title: Therapeutic step according to pediatric GINA
items.contentHtml:
Therapeutic step according to pediatric GINA

  • Step 1: inhaled salbutamol as needed in intermittent asthma; does not require maintenance inhaled corticosteroids.
  • Step 2: add low-dose inhaled corticosteroids as daily maintenance treatment (e.g. budesonide 100-200 mcg/day).
  • Step 3: medium-dose inhaled corticosteroids (budesonide 200-400 mcg/day) plus agonist inhaled long-acting beta2 (formoterol/salmeterol) or leukotriene antagonist (montelukast).
  • Step 4: high-dose inhaled corticosteroids (>400 mcg/day) plus inhaled long-acting beta2 agonist, consider tiotropium or anti-immunoglobulin E monoclonal antibody (omalizumab).
  • Step 5: add oral corticosteroids at the lowest possible dose and refer to a specialist.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis with bronchiolitis and foreign bodies
items.title: Differential diagnosis with bronchiolitis and foreign bodies
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with bronchiolitis and foreign bodies

  • Bronchiolitis: first episode of wheezing in <2 years, associated with respiratory syncytial virus infection, with rhinorrhea, cough and fever; does not respond consistently to beta2 agonists.
  • Foreign body in the airway: sudden onset of cough, stridor or unilateral wheezing, without fever or viral prodromes; on the chest x-ray there may be unilateral air trapping or atelectasis.
  • Clue: foreign body, absence of response to bronchodilators and asymmetry on auscultation; perform bronchoscopy if suspected.
  • Other causes: bronchopulmonary dysplasia in premature infants, cystic fibrosis with recurrent infections, and vascular rings that compress the trachea.
